chunk_summary: Surgical intervention in acute osteoarticular infections is essential when antibiotics fail, diagnostic certainty requires tissue, or complications like abscesses or necrosis arise. Early specialist review is critical for high-risk sites like the hip or spine.
learning_objectives: Understand the indications for surgical intervention in acute osteoarticular infections.
Identify scenarios requiring urgent drainage or debridement in septic arthritis and osteomyelitis.
Recognize the importance of multidisciplinary collaboration in managing complex bone infections.
key_facts: Urgent drainage is mandatory in septic arthritis to prevent cartilage damage.
Surgical or image-guided drainage is indicated for subperiosteal abscess, intramuscular abscess, or large collections.
Failure to improve after 48-72 hours of antibiotics necessitates surgical reassessment.
Delayed drainage in the hip, shoulder, pelvis, or spine increases risks of osteonecrosis, neurologic injury, or disability.
Routine use of local antibiotic beads is not recommended in most pediatric cases.
reference_quote: In septic arthritis, urgent drainage is the default because pus within the joint rapidly injures cartilage. (Source: Indian Academy of Pediatrics Consensus Statement)
document_key_topics: Indications for surgical intervention in bone infections
Management of septic arthritis and osteomyelitis
Role of multidisciplinary teams in infection control
